blocks.text: I’m teaching a beginner Arduino workshop. Design a first project that feels magical in 20 minutes and teaches inputs, outputs, and serial debugging.
The project must reveal cause and effect quickly. A light-reactive RGB bloom has a satisfying physical interaction and useful debugging signals.
Requirements: beginner-friendly, 20 minutes, visible payoff, teaches analog input and PWM output.
Drafted photoresistor input, RGB PWM output, smoothing, and serial diagnostics.
Compile model checks pin constants and map ranges; no blocking delay except 20ms loop cadence.

# handout.md

- Keep the workflow concrete.
- Show the exact command and expected result.

blocks.text: Added LIGHT_MIN/LIGHT_MAX and constrained darkness mapping to avoid flicker in bright rooms.
Created handout with wiring table, photoresistor divider diagram, and first-run checklist.

toolInput.old_string: If it does not work, check your wires.
toolInput.new_string: If the LED stays dark, flip it around first. Then check GND, D3, and the resistor leg one at a time.

blocks.text: Sketch uses 3148 bytes (9%) of program storage space. Global variables use 196 bytes.
light=781 smooth=774 darkness=63 red=20 green=32 blue=91. Values move when the sensor is covered.
Added observation-first debugging: numbers move but LED does not, LED lights but backwards, values stuck at 0/1023.

Project: Night Garden. Students cover a photoresistor with their hand and an RGB LED blooms brighter as the room gets darker. They learn analog input, PWM output, mapping ranges, and why Serial Monitor is their friend.
Make the troubleshooting section friendly. People will wire the LED backwards.

blocks.text: Added slide sequence: electricity loop, sensor divider, PWM bloom, serial monitor as evidence.
Confirmed each kit has one RGB LED, 220 ohm resistors, 10k resistor, photoresistor, button, and jumpers.
Added INPUT_PULLUP button on D2 to freeze current color for fast students.
[Request interrupted by user]
Actually keep it short, but add a stretch goal for fast students.

# facilitator notes.md

blocks.text: Compile passes after stretch-goal button state was added.
Freeze mode holds RGB values and serial prints frozen=true until button is released.
Added pacing notes and two rescue paths: use Serial first, then swap LED polarity.

Stretch goal: add a button that freezes the current garden color. This introduces digital input, pull-up mode, and state. Bonus: hold the button for two seconds to enter calibration mode and record the room’s normal light level.
